category.text: $course$/Advanced Algebra (M.Sc. Mathematics)/CSIR PYQ — Unit II — Rings, ideals, polynomial rings and UFD

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q1 — Z[x] is UFD but not PID

questiontext.text: Which of the following rings is a UFD but NOT a PID?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\).
\(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is a UFD (since \(\mathbb{Z}\) is a UFD and UFD is preserved under adjoining a variable). It is NOT a PID because the ideal \((2, x)=\{2f(x)+xg(x):f,g\in\mathbb{Z}[x]\}\) is not principal. \(\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\mathbb{Z}[i]\) are PIDs (in fact Euclidean domains). \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is also a PID.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q2 — Ideal (2,x) in Z[x]

questiontext.text: In the ring \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\), the ideal \((2,x)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Maximal.
There is a surjective ring homomorphism \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\to\mathbb{F}_2\) (send \(x\mapsto 0\), reduce coefficients mod 2). Its kernel is exactly \((2,x)\). Since \(\mathbb{F}_2=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) is a field, \((2,x)\) is a maximal (and hence prime) ideal.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q3 — Z/nZ is field

questiontext.text: The ring \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) is a field if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(n\) is prime.
If \(n=p\) is prime, every non-zero element in \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is invertible (by Bezout), making it a field. If \(n\) is composite, say \(n=ab\) with \(1

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q4 — Eisenstein's criterion

questiontext.text: Eisenstein's irreducibility criterion states that \(f(x)=a_nx^n+\cdots+a_0\in\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\) if there exists a prime \(p\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(p\mid a_i\) for \(0\le i\le n-1\), \(p mid a_n\), and \(p^2 mid a_0\).
Under these conditions, if \(f=gh\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), reducing mod \(p\) gives \( ar{f}= ar{a}_n x^n\) and both \( ar{g}, ar{h}\) must be monomials. A constant-term argument using \(p^2 mid a_0\) gives a contradiction.

answer.text: \(p\mid a_i\) for \(0\le i\le n-1\), \(p\nmid a_n\), \(p^2\nmid a_0\)

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q5 — Irreducibility of x⁴+1 over Q

questiontext.text: The polynomial \(x^4+1\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
Substitute \(x\to x+1\): \((x+1)^4+1=x^4+4x^3+6x^2+4x+2\). By Eisenstein with \(p=2\): \(2\mid 4,6,4,2\) (all non-leading coefficients), \(2\nmid 1\) (leading), and \(4\nmid 2\). So \(x^4+1\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

answer.text: Reducible as a product of two quadratics over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q6 — Number of maximal ideals in Z/12Z

questiontext.text: The number of maximal ideals in the ring \(\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 2.
Maximal ideals of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) correspond to prime divisors of \(n\). Since \(12=2^2\cdot 3\), the prime divisors are 2 and 3. The maximal ideals are \((2)=\{0,2,4,6,8,10\}\) and \((3)=\{0,3,6,9\}\), giving exactly 2 maximal ideals.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q7 — Prime ideal characterisation

questiontext.text: In a commutative ring \(R\) with unity, an ideal \(P\) is prime if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(R/P\) is an integral domain.
By definition, \(P\) is prime iff \(ab\in P\Rightarrow a\in P\) or \(b\in P\). This is equivalent to \(R/P\) having no zero divisors, i.e., \(R/P\) is an integral domain.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q8 — Maximal ideal characterisation

questiontext.text: In a commutative ring \(R\) with unity, an ideal \(M\) is maximal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(R/M\) is a field.
\(M\) is maximal iff there is no ideal strictly between \(M\) and \(R\), iff \(R/M\) has no non-trivial ideals, iff \(R/M\) is a field. Since every field is an integral domain, every maximal ideal is prime (in a commutative ring with unity).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q9 — Z[√−5] is not UFD

questiontext.text: Which of the following rings is NOT a unique factorisation domain (UFD)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\).
In \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\), the element 6 has two factorisations: \(6=2\cdot 3=(1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})\). One can check that \(2,3,1\pm\sqrt{-5}\) are all irreducible but not prime in this ring, violating unique factorisation.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q10 — x²+1 irreducibility

questiontext.text: Over which of the following fields is \(x^2+1\) irreducible?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{R}\).
Over \(\mathbb{F}_2\): \(1^2+1=0\), so \(x=1\) is a root — reducible. Over \(\mathbb{F}_5\): \(2^2+1=5\equiv 0\), root exists — reducible. Over \(\mathbb{C}\): \(x^2+1=(x+i)(x-i)\) — reducible. Over \(\mathbb{R}\): discriminant \(=-4<0\), no real roots and degree 2, so irreducible.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q11 — (x²+1) in R[x]

questiontext.text: The ideal \((x^2+1)\) in \(\mathbb{R}[x]\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Maximal.
\(\mathbb{R}[x]/(x^2+1)\cong\mathbb{C}\) (since \(x^2+1\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{R}\)). Since \(\mathbb{C}\) is a field, \((x^2+1)\) is a maximal ideal. Every maximal ideal in a commutative ring is also prime.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q12 — Nilradical

questiontext.text: The nilradical of a commutative ring \(R\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: The intersection of all prime ideals of \(R\).
The nilradical \(\mathfrak{N}(R)=\{r\in R:r^n=0\text{ for some }n>0\}\) is the set of all nilpotent elements. By a standard theorem (using Zorn's Lemma), \(\mathfrak{N}(R)=\bigcap_{\mathfrak{p}\text{ prime}}\mathfrak{p}\).

answer.text: The intersection of all prime ideals of \(R\)
The intersection of all maximal ideals of \(R\)
The set of all zero divisors

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q13 — Ideal (x) in Z[x]

questiontext.text: The ideal \((x)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Prime but not maximal.
\(\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}\), which is an integral domain but not a field. So \((x)\) is prime (not maximal). The ideal \((x,2)\supset(x)\) strictly, confirming \((x)\) is not maximal.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q14 — PID implies UFD

questiontext.text: In the hierarchy of integral domains, which implication is correct?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Euclidean domain \(\Rightarrow\) PID \(\Rightarrow\) UFD \(\Rightarrow\) Integral domain.
Every Euclidean domain is a PID (the ideal generated by any element of smallest Euclidean value works). Every PID is a UFD (ascending chain condition holds + irreducibles are prime). Not every UFD is a PID (\(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is a counter-example).

answer.text: Euclidean domain \(\Rightarrow\) PID \(\Rightarrow\) UFD \(\Rightarrow\) Integral domain

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q15 — Gauss's Lemma

questiontext.text: Gauss's Lemma states that a primitive polynomial \(f(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is irreducible in \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(f(x)\) is irreducible in \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\).
A polynomial is primitive if its coefficients have gcd 1. Gauss's Lemma: the product of two primitive polynomials is primitive. Consequence: if \(f\) is primitive, then \(f\) is irreducible in \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) iff it is irreducible in \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\). This allows Eisenstein's criterion (applied over \(\mathbb{Z}\)) to prove irreducibility over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q16 — Characteristic of Z_p × Z_q

questiontext.text: The characteristic of the ring \(\mathbb{Z}_p\times\mathbb{Z}_q\) where \(p\ne q\) are distinct primes is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(pq\).
The characteristic of a product ring \(R_1\times R_2\) is \(\mathrm{lcm}(\mathrm{char}(R_1),\mathrm{char}(R_2))\). Here \(\mathrm{char}(\mathbb{Z}_p)=p\) and \(\mathrm{char}(\mathbb{Z}_q)=q\). Since \(\gcd(p,q)=1\), \(\mathrm{lcm}(p,q)=pq\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q17 — Ideals of Z₄

questiontext.text: The total number of ideals in \(\mathbb{Z}_4=\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 3.
Ideals of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) correspond to divisors of \(n\). Divisors of 4 are 1, 2, 4, giving ideals \(\{0\}\), \(\{0,2\}\), and \(\mathbb{Z}_4\). So there are exactly 3 ideals. Compare: \(\mathbb{Z}_6\) has 4 ideals (divisors 1,2,3,6).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q18 — Union of ideals

questiontext.text: If \(I\) and \(J\) are ideals of a commutative ring \(R\), which of the following is NOT necessarily an ideal of \(R\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(I\cup J\).
\(I+J\), \(I\cap J\), and \(IJ\) are always ideals. However \(I\cup J\) is an ideal iff \(I\subseteq J\) or \(J\subseteq I\). Counterexample: in \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(2\mathbb{Z}\cup 3\mathbb{Z}\) is not an ideal since \(2,3\in 2\mathbb{Z}\cup 3\mathbb{Z}\) but \(2+3=5\notin 2\mathbb{Z}\cup 3\mathbb{Z}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q19 — F[x] over a field is Euclidean

questiontext.text: The polynomial ring \(F[x]\) over a field \(F\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: A Euclidean domain (with the degree as the Euclidean norm).
For any \(f,g\in F[x]\) with \(g\ne 0\), the division algorithm gives \(f=qg+r\) with \(\deg r<\deg g\). This makes \(F[x]\) a Euclidean domain, hence a PID, hence a UFD. Every irreducible polynomial generates a maximal ideal in \(F[x]\).

answer.text: A UFD but not a PID

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q20 — Noetherian ring

questiontext.text: A commutative ring \(R\) is Noetherian if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Every ascending chain of ideals stabilises (ACC).
The ACC (Ascending Chain Condition) states: for every chain \(I_1\subseteq I_2\subseteq\cdots\), there exists \(N\) such that \(I_n=I_N\) for all \(n\ge N\). Equivalently, every ideal of \(R\) is finitely generated (Hilbert's basis theorem: \(R\) Noetherian \(\Rightarrow R[x]\) Noetherian).

answer.text: Every ascending chain of ideals stabilises
Every descending chain of ideals stabilises

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q21 — Irreducibility of x³−2 over Q

questiontext.text: The polynomial \(x^3-2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
By Eisenstein's criterion with \(p=2\): \(2\mid(-2)\), \(4\nmid(-2)\), and \(2\nmid 1\) (leading coefficient). So \(x^3-2\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\). The root \(\sqrt[3]{2}\) generates an extension \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})\) of degree 3 over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

answer.text: Reducible as product of three linear factors over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q22 — Finite integral domain is a field

questiontext.text: Every finite integral domain is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: A field.
Let \(R\) be a finite integral domain and \(a\ne 0\in R\). The map \(x\mapsto ax\) is injective (no zero divisors) and hence bijective (finite set). So there exists \(b\in R\) with \(ab=1\), i.e., \(a\) is invertible. This is Wedderburn's theorem for commutative rings.

answer.text: A UFD but not necessarily a field
A PID but not necessarily a field
Noetherian but not necessarily a field

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q23 — Hilbert Basis Theorem

questiontext.text: Hilbert's Basis Theorem states that if \(R\) is a Noetherian ring, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(R[x]\) is also Noetherian.
Hilbert's Basis Theorem: if \(R\) is Noetherian, then the polynomial ring \(R[x]\) is Noetherian. By induction, \(R[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) is Noetherian. In particular, since \(\mathbb{Z}\) and any field are Noetherian, so are \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) and \(k[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q24 — Nilpotent element

questiontext.text: An element \(a\) in a ring \(R\) is called nilpotent if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(a^n=0\) for some positive integer \(n\).
In \(\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}\), the element \(6\) satisfies \(6^2=36\equiv 0\pmod{12}\), so 6 is nilpotent. The set of all nilpotent elements forms an ideal (the nilradical) in a commutative ring.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q25 — UFD: irreducible iff prime

questiontext.text: In a UFD, an element is irreducible if and only if it is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Prime.
In any integral domain, every prime is irreducible. In a UFD the converse holds: every irreducible is prime. This is characteristic of UFDs and fails in non-UFDs (e.g., in \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\), the element 2 is irreducible but not prime since \(2\mid 6=(1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})\) but \(2\nmid 1\pm\sqrt{-5}\)).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q26 — Cyclotomic polynomial Φ_p(x)

questiontext.text: For a prime \(p\), the cyclotomic polynomial \(\Phi_p(x)=x^{p-1}+x^{p-2}+\cdots+x+1\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).
Substituting \(x\to x+1\): \(\Phi_p(x+1)=\frac{(x+1)^p-1}{x}=x^{p-1}+px^{p-2}+\binom{p}{2}x^{p-3}+\cdots+p\). By Eisenstein with the prime \(p\): all non-leading coefficients are divisible by \(p\), the constant term is \(p\) (not divisible by \(p^2\)). So \(\Phi_p(x)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q27 — Chinese Remainder Theorem

questiontext.text: If \(m,n\) are coprime positive integers, then by the Chinese Remainder Theorem:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\mathbb{Z}/mn\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) as rings.
When \(\gcd(m,n)=1\), the ideals \(m\mathbb{Z}\) and \(n\mathbb{Z}\) are comaximal (\(m\mathbb{Z}+n\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{Z}\)) and their product \(mn\mathbb{Z}=m\mathbb{Z}\cap n\mathbb{Z}\). CRT gives the ring isomorphism \(\mathbb{Z}/mn\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\).

answer.text: The statement requires \(m,n\) to be prime

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q28 — Krull dimension of Z

questiontext.text: The Krull dimension of the ring \(\mathbb{Z}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: 1.
The Krull dimension is the supremum of lengths of chains of prime ideals. In \(\mathbb{Z}\), the chains are \(\{0\}\subset(p)\) for prime \(p\). The longest chain has length 1. So \(\dim\mathbb{Z}=1\). A field has Krull dimension 0; \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) has Krull dimension 2.

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q29 — Z[i] is Euclidean domain

questiontext.text: The ring of Gaussian integers \(\mathbb{Z}[i]=\{a+bi : a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: A Euclidean domain (hence a PID and UFD).
The norm \(N(a+bi)=a^2+b^2\) is a Euclidean function for \(\mathbb{Z}[i]\). Division with remainder: for any \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{Z}[i]\) with \(\beta\ne 0\), write \(\alpha/\beta=x+yi\) in \(\mathbb{Q}[i]\), round to nearest Gaussian integer \(q\), and set \(r=\alpha-q\beta\) with \(N(r)

answer.text: A Euclidean domain (hence PID and UFD)
A UFD but not a PID

name.text: CSIR PYQ Unit II Q30 — Jacobson radical

questiontext.text: The Jacobson radical \(J(R)\) of a commutative ring \(R\) is defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: The intersection of all maximal ideals of \(R\).
\(J(R)=\bigcap\{M:M\text{ maximal ideal of }R\}\). An element \(x\in J(R)\) iff \(1-rx\) is a unit for all \(r\in R\) (Nakayama's lemma uses \(J(R)\)). Compare with the nilradical, which is the intersection of all prime ideals. Since maximal ideals are prime, \(\mathfrak{N}(R)\subseteq J(R)\).

answer.text: The intersection of all maximal ideals of \(R\)
The intersection of all prime ideals of \(R\)
The set of all nilpotent elements
The set of all zero divisors
